Q1: My study routine was honestly all over the place this semester. I tried to dedicate around 20 hours a week, but with my anxiety levels being what they are, I'd often have to break things into smaller chunks. I'm a visual learner, so I'd spend a lot of time creating diagrams and mind maps outside of the LMS. Some weeks I'd be really focused, other weeks I'd struggle to even open my laptop because the stress was overwhelming.
Q2: My pattern varied a lot depending on what was due and how I was feeling mentally. I'd have these bursts of productivity around deadlines, but then periods where I couldn't bring myself to engage at all. The high stress and anxiety I deal with meant that even when I planned to study at regular times, my mental state would often derail those plans. I tried to be consistent, but life and my own head got in the way more than I'd like to admit.
Q3: Usually it was either an assignment deadline looming or the gamified tasks they introduced - those little achievement badges actually motivated me more than I expected. Sometimes I'd log in just to check if there was feedback on something I submitted. Other times it was pure panic when I realized I'd missed something important. The notification emails would sometimes prompt me, though I'll admit I often ignored those when my anxiety was peaking.
Q4: Not really, if I'm being honest. I never got into a comfortable rhythm with the LMS. The gamified elements helped a bit - earning points and seeing progress bars fill up gave me small dopamine hits that kept me coming back. But it never felt automatic or natural. I always had to consciously force myself to log in, which took mental energy I didn't always have.
Q5: The video content was definitely the most useful for me because I'm such a visual learner - I watched over two hours worth of material. I'd pause, rewind, and really absorb the visual explanations. The gamified tasks also helped because they broke things down into manageable pieces, which was crucial for managing my anxiety. Discussion posts were occasionally helpful when other students explained things in ways that clicked better than the lectures.
Q6: I definitely ignored a lot of the reading materials and text-heavy resources. My brain just doesn't process walls of text well, especially when I'm stressed. I also avoided the discussion forums more than I should have because the social aspect of posting made me anxious. Some of the optional supplementary materials I just never got to - there were only so many hours in the day and I had to prioritize what would directly impact my grades.
Q7: An activity felt worth my time if I could see immediate relevance to the exams or assignments. The gamified elements helped here because the point system made even small tasks feel rewarding. Visual content that I could actually understand and apply felt valuable. If something was going to take hours and I couldn't see how it connected to my grade, I'd probably skip it - I had to be strategic with my limited mental energy.
Q8: Required activities carried this weight of obligation that honestly made them more stressful. I'd do them, but often with a lot of anxiety about getting them right. Optional activities felt lower stakes, which sometimes made them more enjoyable, but also easier to skip when I was overwhelmed. The gamified optional tasks were different though - they felt like a game rather than schoolwork, which made them more appealing even when they weren't mandatory.
Q9: Absolutely, a huge portion of my studying happened offline. I'd download videos and watch them on my tablet during commutes. I created extensive visual notes and diagrams by hand - that's really how I learn best. I used external YouTube videos to supplement concepts I didn't fully grasp. I also had textbooks and would sometimes study from those for hours without the LMS knowing anything about it.
Q10: There were many times I'd be studying intensely but the LMS would show nothing. I'd print out lecture slides and annotate them by hand for hours. I'd work through problems on paper, creating visual representations of concepts. Sometimes I'd discuss course material with classmates in person or over WhatsApp. My best learning often happened when I was away from screens, working through things visually on paper.
Q11: Yes, constantly. I'd download a video once, then rewatch it multiple times offline. I'd save PDFs and lecture slides to my tablet and review them repeatedly without logging back in. This was actually my preferred way to study because it meant I could learn without the pressure of being online and seeing all the other things I hadn't done yet. The LMS interface itself sometimes triggered my anxiety.
Q12: There were definitely periods where engagement became really difficult. When my anxiety spiked, which happened frequently, I'd avoid the LMS entirely because just seeing my incomplete tasks made me feel worse. Around midterms when everything piled up, I hit a wall. There were also personal things happening - being 25 and still in university while friends are advancing in careers added pressure that made focusing harder.
Q13: Massively. When I was exhausted or overwhelmed, the LMS became this thing I dreaded opening. Competing responsibilities - work, family expectations, trying to maintain some social life - meant the LMS often got pushed aside. Confusion about certain topics made me avoid those modules entirely rather than face feeling stupid. The fatigue from managing high anxiety meant some days I just couldn't engage at all, even when I wanted to.
Q14: Yeah, there were times I'd scroll through materials without really absorbing anything. I'd have a video playing while my mind was somewhere else entirely, worrying about other things. Sometimes I'd open readings and stare at them, but nothing would stick because my stress levels were too high. Those sessions felt like going through the motions rather than actual learning.
Q15: I postponed assignments constantly, even knowing they were important. The anxiety around doing them 'wrong' or not being good enough made starting feel impossible. I'd tell myself I'd do it tomorrow when I felt better, but that better day didn't always come. It's frustrating because I knew logically what I needed to do, but the emotional barrier was real. My assignment completion rate suffered because of this avoidance pattern.
Q16: Deadlines were basically the only thing that forced me to engage sometimes. I'd have these intense bursts of activity right before something was due. The panic of an approaching deadline would override my anxiety about the work itself. It wasn't healthy - I'd stay up late, stress-eat, and barely make submissions. But without that external pressure, I'd often drift and not engage at all.
Q17: Feedback had a complicated effect on me. Positive feedback boosted my confidence and made me want to engage more. But critical feedback, even when constructive, would sometimes send me into a spiral where I'd avoid the course for days. I performed well on exams, so getting that 83 score validated that my methods were working, which helped. The gamified feedback - points and badges - was easier to handle emotionally than written critiques.
Q18: The course structure was both helpful and challenging. Having 20 online courses meant a lot of flexibility, which I needed, but also meant I had to self-motivate, which was hard with low motivation. The gamified elements were a smart addition that helped me engage when I otherwise wouldn't have. But the sheer volume of content across all those courses was overwhelming and contributed to my incomplete task rate.
Q19: The gamified tasks definitely made engagement easier - they broke things into smaller, achievable goals which worked well for my anxiety. Visual content was accessible for my learning style. However, the discussion requirements made engagement harder because social interaction online stresses me out. The lack of clear prioritization of what was most important versus optional made it hard to know where to focus my limited energy.
Q20: I think that could be someone like me on a bad week - logging in repeatedly, clicking around, watching videos, but being too anxious or overwhelmed to actually complete and submit work. It could also be someone who's genuinely trying to understand material but struggling with the actual assignments. Or maybe they're doing the learning but their offline work isn't translating to submitted assignments. The LMS shows presence, not productivity.
Q21: That actually sounds like my situation to some degree. I got Merit with an 83 on exams despite relatively low LMS engagement because I did a lot of learning offline. I'm proof that LMS activity doesn't equal learning. Some people are efficient - they engage deeply but briefly. Others, like me, do significant work that simply isn't captured by login frequencies and click counts.
Q22: Course logs can show patterns - when someone logs in, how long they spend, what they click on. They can flag students who disappear entirely. But they miss the quality of engagement, the offline studying, the mental state behind the clicks. They can't tell if someone is staring blankly at a video or deeply processing it. They miss the anxiety, the struggle, the real human experience of learning.
Q23: Being engaged means actually caring about understanding the material, not just completing tasks. It's when your brain is actively processing and connecting ideas, whether you're online or sitting with a notebook. For me, engagement often looks like drawing diagrams and thinking deeply about concepts - none of which shows up in any log. It's about mental investment, not just physical presence in a system.
Q24: The LMS wouldn't show my anxiety levels or how much mental energy it took just to log in some days. It wouldn't capture the hours I spent creating visual study materials by hand. It wouldn't show the peer discussions over coffee or the YouTube videos that finally made concepts click. It wouldn't reflect that despite everything working against me - the stress, the anxiety, the low motivation - I still pushed through and earned Merit. That resilience doesn't show up in any data.
